crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION.full_description:
Allows the app to get your
      precise location using the Global Positioning System (GPS) or network
      location sources such as cell towers and Wi-Fi. These location services
      must be turned on and available to your device for the app to use them.
      Apps may use this to determine where you are, and may consume additional
      battery power.

android.permission.INTERNET.full_description:
Allows the app to create
     network sockets and use custom network protocols. The browser and other
     applications provide means to send data to the internet, so this
     permission is not required to send data to the internet.

android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to view
      information about network connections such as which networks exist and are
      connected.

android.permission.ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATIO.full_description:
Allows the app to get your
      approximate location. This location is derived by location services using
      network location sources such as cell towers and Wi-Fi. These location
      services must be turned on and available to your device for the app to
      use them. Apps may use this to determine approximately where you
      are.

android.permission.READ_PHONE_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to access the phone
      features of the device.  This permission allows the app to determine the
      phone number and device IDs, whether a call is active, and the remote number
      connected by a call.

android.permission.WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAG.short_description: modify or delete the contents of your SD card
android.permission.WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAG.full_description: Allows the app to write to the SD card.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Potential Raspberry Robin Registry Set Internet Settings ZoneMap
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects registry modifications related to the proxy configuration of the system, potentially associated with the Raspberry Robin malware, as seen in campaigns running in Q1 2024.
Raspberry Robin may alter proxy settings to circumvent security measures, ensuring unhindered connection with Command and Control servers for maintaining control over compromised systems if there are any proxy settings that are blocking connections.

android.permission.VIBRATE.full_description: Allows the application to control the vibrator.

android.permission.RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETE.full_description: Allows an application to start itself as soon as the system has finished booting. This can make it take longer to start the phone and allow the application to slow down the overall phone by always running.

android.permission.INTERNET.full_description: Allows an application to create network sockets.

android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE.full_description: Allows an application to view the status of all networks.

android.permission.ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATIO.full_description: Access coarse location sources, such as the mobile network database, to determine an approximate phone location, where available. Malicious applications can use this to determine approximately where you are.

android.permission.WAKE_LOCK.full_description: Allows an application to prevent the phone from going to sleep.

android.permission.ACCESS_WIFI_STATE.full_description: Allows an application to view the information about the status of Wi-Fi.

android.permission.READ_PHONE_STATE.full_description: Allows the application to access the phone features of the device. An application with this permission can determine the phone number and serial number of this phone, whether a call is active, the number that call is connected to and so on.

android.permission.WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAG.full_description: Allows an application to write to the SD card.

android.permission.BROADCAST_STICKY.full_description: Allows an application to send sticky broadcasts, which remain after the broadcast ends. Malicious applications can make the phone slow or unstable by causing it to use too much memory.

android.permission.GET_ACCOUNTS.full_description: Allows an application to access the list of accounts known by the phone.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious Double Extension File Execution
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects suspicious use of an .exe extension after a non-executable file extension like .pdf.exe, a set of spaces or underlines to cloak the executable file in spear phishing campaigns
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Florian Roth (Nextron Systems), @blu3_team (idea), Nasreddine Bencherchali (Nextron Systems)

sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects suspicious DNS queries to Monero mining pools

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Rare Remote Thread Creation By Uncommon Source Image
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects uncommon processes creating remote threads.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: New RUN Key Pointing to Suspicious Folder
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects suspicious new RUN key element pointing to an executable in a suspicious folder
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Florian Roth (Nextron Systems), Markus Neis, Sander Wiebing

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Disable Windows Defender Functionalities Via Registry Keys
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects when attackers or tools disable Windows Defender functionalities via the Windows registry
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: AlertIQ, Ján Trenčanský, frack113, Nasreddine Bencherchali, Swachchhanda Shrawan Poudel

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Driver Load From A Temporary Directory
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects a driver load from a temporary directory

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Detects URL shortcut execution (RogueURL campaign)
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects malicious URL shortcut execution

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Vulnerable WinRing0 Driver Load
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects the load of a signed WinRing0 driver often used by threat actors, crypto miners (XMRIG) or malware for privilege escalation

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Service Binary in Suspicious Folder
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detect the creation of a service with a service binary located in a suspicious directory

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Script Interpreter Execution From Suspicious Folder
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects a suspicious script execution in temporary folders or folders accessible by environment variables
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Florian Roth (Nextron Systems), Nasreddine Bencherchali (Nextron Systems)

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious Windows Service Tampering
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects the usage of binaries such as 'net', 'sc' or 'powershell' in order to stop, pause, disable or delete critical or important Windows services such as AV, Backup, etc. As seen being used in some ransomware scripts

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious Script Execution From Temp Folder
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects a suspicious script executions from temporary folder
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Florian Roth (Nextron Systems), Max Altgelt (Nextron Systems), Tim Shelton

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious PowerShell Parent Process
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects a suspicious or uncommon parent processes of PowerShell

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious MSHTA Child Process
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects a suspicious process spawning from an "mshta.exe" process, which could be indicative of a malicious HTA script execution

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Outbound Network Connection Initiated By Microsoft Dialer
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects outbound network connection initiated by Microsoft Dialer.
The Microsoft Dialer, also known as Phone Dialer, is a built-in utility application included in various versions of the Microsoft Windows operating system. Its primary function is to provide users with a graphical interface for managing phone calls via a modem or a phone line connected to the computer.
This is an outdated process in the current conext of it's usage and is a common target for info stealers for process injection, and is used to make C2 connections, common example is "Rhadamanthys"

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Vulnerable Driver Load
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects loading of known vulnerable drivers via their hash.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: PowerShell DownloadFile
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects the execution of powershell, a WebClient object creation and the invocation of DownloadFile in a single command line

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Schedule Task Creation From Env Variable Or Potentially Suspicious Path Via Schtasks.EXE
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects Schtask creations that point to a suspicious folder or an environment variable often used by malware

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious PowerShell Download - Powershell Script
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects suspicious PowerShell download command

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Wow6432Node CurrentVersion Autorun Keys Modification
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects modification of autostart extensibility point (ASEP) in registry.
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Victor Sergeev, Daniil Yugoslavskiy, Gleb Sukhodolskiy, Timur Zinniatullin, oscd.community, Tim Shelton, frack113 (split)

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: PowerShell Download Pattern
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects a Powershell process that contains download commands in its command line string
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Florian Roth (Nextron Systems), oscd.community, Jonhnathan Ribeiro

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Use Short Name Path in Command Line
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detect use of the Windows 8.3 short name. Which could be used as a method to avoid command-line detection

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Usage Of Web Request Commands And Cmdlets - ScriptBlock
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects the use of various web request commands with commandline tools and Windows PowerShell cmdlets (including aliases) via PowerShell scriptblock logs

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Remote Thread Created In Shell Application
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects remote thread creation in command shell applications, such as "Cmd.EXE" and "PowerShell.EXE".
It is a common technique used by malware, such as IcedID, to inject malicious code and execute it within legitimate processes.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Uncommon Child Process Of Conhost.EXE
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects uncommon "conhost" child processes. This could be a sign of "conhost" usage as a LOLBIN or potential process injection activity.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Powershell Defender Exclusion
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects requests to exclude files, folders or processes from Antivirus scanning using PowerShell cmdlets

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: CurrentVersion Autorun Keys Modification
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects modification of autostart extensibility point (ASEP) in registry.
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Victor Sergeev, Daniil Yugoslavskiy, Gleb Sukhodolskiy, Timur Zinniatullin, oscd.community, Tim Shelton, frack113 (split)

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious Msbuild Execution By Uncommon Parent Process

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Uncommon Svchost Parent Process
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects an uncommon svchost parent process

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Potential Binary Or Script Dropper Via PowerShell
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects PowerShell creating a binary executable or a script file.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Disable Tamper Protection on Windows Defender
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects disabling Windows Defender Tamper Protection

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Windows Defender Exclusions Added - PowerShell
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects modifications to the Windows Defender configuration settings using PowerShell to add exclusions

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: PSScriptPolicyTest Creation By Uncommon Process
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects the creation of the "PSScriptPolicyTest" PowerShell script by an uncommon process. This file is usually generated by Microsoft Powershell to test against Applocker.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: PowerShell Web Download
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects suspicious ways to download files or content using PowerShell

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Usage Of Web Request Commands And Cmdlets

sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects non-interactive PowerShell activity by looking at the "powershell" process with a non-user GUI process such as "explorer.exe" as a parent.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects the creation of scheduled tasks by user accounts via the "schtasks" utility.

android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION.full_description:
Allows the app to get your
      precise location using the Global Positioning System (GPS) or network
      location sources such as cell towers and Wi-Fi. These location services
      must be turned on and available to your device for the app to use them.
      Apps may use this to determine where you are, and may consume additional
      battery power.

android.permission.VIBRATE.full_description: Allows the app to control the vibrator.

android.permission.RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETE.full_description:
Allows the app to
        have itself started as soon as the system has finished booting.
        This can make it take longer to start the phone and allow the
        app to slow down the overall phone by always running.

android.permission.INTERNET.full_description:
Allows the app to create
     network sockets and use custom network protocols. The browser and other
     applications provide means to send data to the internet, so this
     permission is not required to send data to the internet.

android.permission.ACCESS_WIFI_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to view information
      about Wi-Fi networking, such as whether Wi-Fi is enabled and name of
      connected Wi-Fi devices.

android.permission.ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATIO.full_description:
Allows the app to get your
      approximate location. This location is derived by location services using
      network location sources such as cell towers and Wi-Fi. These location
      services must be turned on and available to your device for the app to
      use them. Apps may use this to determine approximately where you
      are.

android.permission.WAKE_LOCK.full_description: Allows the app to prevent the phone from going to sleep.

android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to view
      information about network connections such as which networks exist and are
      connected.

android.permission.READ_PHONE_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to access the phone
      features of the device.  This permission allows the app to determine the
      phone number and device IDs, whether a call is active, and the remote number
      connected by a call.

android.permission.BROADCAST_STICKY.full_description:
Allows the app to
    send sticky broadcasts, which remain after the broadcast ends. Excessive
    use may make the phone slow or unstable by causing it to use too
    much memory.

android.permission.GET_ACCOUNTS.full_description:
Allows the app to get
      the list of accounts known by the phone.  This may include any accounts
      created by applications you have installed.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This ELF binary is a multi-functional botnet agent featuring DDoS capabilities, cryptojacking, and extensive anti-forensic techniques. It masquerades as a kernel thread ('[kworker/0:1]') and employs environment checks to detect debuggers (GDB, strace, IDA) and virtualization (VMware/VirtualBox MAC prefixes). The sample exhibits rootkit-like behavior by downloading a replacement utility from 'botnet.world' to overwrite standard system tools (/usr/bin/ss, /usr/bin/netstat, /usr/bin/lsof), likely to hide its malicious network activity. It communicates with C2 domains (botnet.world, backup1.botnet.world) to receive attack commands, including a wide array of flood methods (SYN, UDP, ICMP, Game-specific, and HTTP-layer floods). Furthermore, it contains a dedicated routine to download and execute the XMRig cryptocurrency miner targeting 'pool.supportxmr.com'.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This PowerPC ELF is a multifunctional botnet agent and cryptominer. It employs masquerading by renaming its process to '[kworker/0:1]' and includes anti-analysis routines to detect monitoring tools like strace, ltrace, and frida. The sample retrieves system metadata and public IP addresses (via api.ipify.org) to exfiltrate to its C2 infrastructure. Key malicious behaviors include a downloader module targeting 'https://main.botnet.world/xmrig' for cryptojacking and a comprehensive DDoS engine supporting numerous attack vectors such as SYN, ACK, UDP, and game-specific floods (Raknet, Valve, Minecraft). IOCs include the domain 'botnet.world' and the use of /tmp/.sh_upd for staging updates.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
Package: unknown (pypi)
Version: N/A
Description: N/A (no METADATA file found)

The package provides a pure-C HTML5 parser with Python bindings. The source code includes standard build configurations (Autotools, GYP, CI files like Travis and AppVeyor), Python CTypes bindings, and adapters for `html5lib` and `BeautifulSoup`. Utility scripts (`gentags.py`, `genperf.py`) are used to generate C and Python code during the build process, which is a legitimate development practice. The `setup.py` custom command correctly handles the copying of the compiled C library for Python packaging. While benchmark HTML files contain external URLs, these are test data and not executed by the package's core logic. There are no indications of malicious intent such as unauthorized system access, data exfiltration, or obfuscation. The project appears to be a well-maintained, legitimate open-source library.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Load Of RstrtMgr.DLL By An Uncommon Process
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects the load of RstrtMgr DLL (Restart Manager) by an uncommon process.
This library has been used during ransomware campaigns to kill processes that would prevent file encryption by locking them (e.g. Conti ransomware, Cactus ransomware). It has also recently been seen used by the BiBi wiper for Windows.
It could also be used for anti-analysis purposes by shut downing specific processes.

Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The analysis of the PDF's internal structure reveals no evidence of malicious behavior. The document lacks any automatic execution triggers, such as /OpenAction, or embedded scripts, like JavaScript, which would be necessary to form a malicious execution chain. While the file exhibits a large number of streams with decoding errors and contains 92 URIs, these anomalies are not associated with any active malicious function. The errors are likely due to file corruption, and the URIs are passive content without any action to launch them.

From a visual perspective, the document is a news and commentary digest from sources including "X22 Report" and "radiopatriot.net". All visible URLs, brand references, and logos appear authentic to the cited sources and are not used in a deceptive manner. There are no phishing elements, such as forms requesting credentials, or attempts to impersonate trusted entities. The content, while highly opinionated and using alarmist language, does not constitute a social engineering attack.

Synthesizing both reports, the final judgment is that the file is benign. The technical analysis confirms the absence of a malicious payload or execution mechanism, and the visual analysis confirms the absence of phishing or deceptive intent. The file's structural issues and its biased content do not represent a direct security threat.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The technical analysis identified significant structural anomalies, including a very high object count and 445 streams that could not be decoded. These issues suggest the file may be corrupted or was created using a non-standard process. However, the analysis crucially found no automatic execution triggers, such as /OpenAction or document-level JavaScript. In the absence of an execution vector, any potential code within the undecodable streams is rendered inert, as it has no mechanism to run.

This assessment is supported by the visual layer analysis, which found the document to be a legitimate ebook from the publisher Farrar, Straus and Giroux (Macmillan). All visible branding, text, and URLs appear authentic and consistent with the publisher. The document contains no social engineering tactics, urgency cues, or other red flags typically associated with malicious content.

Considering both reports, the file is determined to be benign. crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The document presents itself as an authentic guest complaint notification from Booking.com, targeting hotel staff. It uses professional branding and creates a sense of urgency, instructing the recipient to click a "View Complaint" button to resolve the issue promptly. This visual presentation serves as a social engineering lure to encourage interaction.

While the visual layer appears legitimate at first glance, it contains significant red flags indicative of a phishing attempt. These include a generic greeting ("Dear staff,") and, most critically, a large block of nonsensical text appended after the footer. This text padding is a well-known evasion technique used by attackers to bypass security filters. The technical analysis corroborates this suspicion by identifying an `/OpenAction` element within the PDF's structure, which is configured to execute a command automatically upon opening the file.

Synthesizing these findings, the document is a malicious phishing lure. The social engineering tactics are designed to trick the user into opening the file and interacting with it, at which point the embedded `/OpenAction` or a hidden link behind the "View Complaint" button would likely execute a malicious payload, such as navigating to a credential harvesting site or downloading further malware. The presence of both a technical trigger mechanism and classic visual-layer evasion techniques provides strong evidence of malicious intent.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
